Dear ML Lakrltz:
This is in response to your letter to the Commission
dated December 30, 1975.
since you! request does not raise
nuhstantial questions under the Political Reform Act, no
formal opinion will be issued.
1 hope, however. the follow»
ing informetion will be helpfu1.
The provisions of the Political Reform Act prohibit
participation in decisions by public officials when it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
W
c generally, on a financial interest of the public
official.
Based upon tET-e
acts you have recited, it does
not appear that the decision to which you allude will have
a financial effect on any financial interest you have.
To
the contrary, the effect of the decision seems to be limited
to non-economic repercussions on me school and its students.
Accordingly. the Political Reform Act does not prohibit your
participation in the decision.
Thank you for your expression of interest in your duties
under the Political Reform Act.
If you have any questions,
please do not hesitate to call me.
Sincerely,
,
Delbert Spurlock. Chief

Dear sire:
I would appreciate an answer to my question:
"would
a conflict of interest exist it, as a city councilman,
I
voted, on my employer's request,
to close part of a city
street?”.
Details:
I am currently employed by the aanforu

Projects and Special Education.
I am also a city council—
man.
The o‘chool Board has requested that the city close a
portion of a street that separates our major part or the

The street is 1.0- wide,
no parking, however,
the Board believes that when the street
is closed, students would be protected as they walk across
the street to class, as well as reducing the number of

The city attorney
believes that

sections.question: Would a conflict of interest exist if, as a city councilman, I voted, on my employer's request, to close part of a city street?
sections.conclusion: The provisions of the Political Reform Act prohibit participation in decisions by public officials when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on a financial interest of the public official. Based upon the facts recited, it does not appear that the decision will have a financial effect on any financial interest. The effect seems to be limited to non-economic repercussions on the school and its students. Accordingly, the Political Reform Act does not prohibit your participation in the decision.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city councilman employed by a school district would have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act if he voted to close a street at his employer's request, when the primary effects would be non-economic benefits to the school and students.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit the requestor's participation because the street closure decision would not have a material financial effect on his financial interests, but rather would only produce non-economic benefits to the school and students.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (dated December 30, 1975) and FPPC response (dated January 21, 1975, likely a typo for 1976). The response is brief and informal, noting no formal opinion would be issued. Minor OCR artifacts present but key content is clear. Extracted from the formal FPPC response portion.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Would a conflict of interest exist if, as a city councilman, I voted, on my employer's request, to close part of a city street?

CONCLUSION: The provisions of the Political Reform Act prohibit participation in decisions by public officials when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on a financial interest of the public official. Based upon the facts recited, it does not appear that the decision will have a financial effect on any financial interest. The effect seems to be limited to non-economic repercussions on the school and its students. Accordingly, the Political Reform Act does not prohibit your participation in the decision.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to a city councilman's inquiry about whether voting to close a street at his school district employer's request would constitute a conflict of interest. The FPPC concluded no conflict exists because the decision would have only non-economic effects.
